title: Agentic Coding in Xcode: A First Step in Implementation (Part 1): From Concept to Runnable Environment
description: A hands-on roadmap based on Xcode 26.3 and Gemini CLI: You will go from 0 to 1 through environment setup, MCP connection and your first executable session.
content:
TL;DRApple is...AI-assisted R&DTaking Agentic Coding to new heights. This article is the first part of the "Agentic Coding in Xcode Implementation Guide" trilogy, focusing on rapid deployment. It guides you step-by-step through getting the underlying MCP (Model Context Protocol) link between Xcode 26.3 and Gemini CLI (>0.27.3) running on your local machine, establishing the first step from "chat AI" to "executable intelligent agent".

Foreword

This is the first in a three-part series, inspired by an article by Peter Friese (link at the end), but the content will be rewritten in a more localized Chinese version incorporating my own practical experience. The key points are:You can get it running on your machine today.。

Series Navigation:

  • Part 1 (This Article): From Concept to Environmental Preparation
  • Part 2: From Prompt to Runnable Demo (Newton’s Apple)
  • Part 3: Common Pitfalls, Compatibility Issues, and Team Implementation Checklist

Why should we focus on Agentic Coding now?

Xcode 26.3 upgrades "AI-assisted coding" from chat to "Agent collaboration":

  • It not only provides suggestions, but also allows for modifications tailored to the project context.
  • It can trigger a build and continue iteratively fixing errors.
  • Connecting external agents with Xcode capabilities through MCP (Model Context Protocol).

Simply put, it used to be "you write, AI reads", but now it's more like "you set the goal, the agent runs the process".

Prerequisites (Don't skip this step)

To reproduce this process, it is recommended that the following version be met:

  1. Xcode 26.3
  2. Gemini CLI >= 0.27.3

Why can't the Gemini version be too low?

  • In early versions of Xcode 26.3, bridged returns,structuredContentThere have been compatibility issues.
  • Subsequent patches to the Gemini CLI have addressed this type of response (see the references at the end of the article for the fix).

Install Gemini CLI:

npm install -g @google/gemini-cli
gemini --version

Open the MCP entry point in Xcode.

Path (macOS):

  1. Open Xcode
  2. Xcode > Settings...(shortcut key ⌘,）
  3. Enter Intelligence
  4. exist Model Context ProtocolArea OpenXcode Tools


Image: Enable MCP support in Xcode Settings > Intelligence (Source: peterfriese.dev)

This step is crucial: if it is not enabled, even if the CLI is configured correctly, you will not be able to see Xcode's tool capabilities.

Connect the Gemini CLI to the Xcode MCP Bridge

Navigate to your project root directory and execute:

gemini mcp add xcode-tools xcrun mcpbridge

This will generate (or update) configuration files within the project:

  • .gemini/settings.json

We recommend configuring it "by project" rather than enabling it globally:

  • Enable only in repositories that require Xcode capabilities.
  • Avoid prompting for authorization confirmation every time you open the CLI
  • Cleaner switching between multiple projects, clearer permission boundaries

Start a minimum feasible session

In this order:

  1. First open the Xcode project
  2. Then execute in the project root directorygemini
  3. Accept Xcode's external tool authorization pop-up


Image: Xcode will pop up a permission confirmation window upon first connection (Source: peterfriese.dev)

You can first check if MCP is working properly within the CLI:

/mcp list
/mcp desc


Image: After a successful connection, the CLI should be able to list xcode-tools (Source: peterfriese.dev)

If you can seexcode-toolsThe tool description and its instructions indicate that the link has been established.

The mental model you should form

The essence of this link is:

graph LR
    A[Gemini CLI] <--"MCP Protocol<br>(JSON-RPC)"--> B(xcrun mcpbridge)
    B <--"Xcode Internal API"--> C[Xcode Tools]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff,stroke:#666,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

in:

  • Gemini is responsible for understanding the goals and planning the steps.
  • Xcode provides engineering-level capabilities (build, context, diagnostics).
  • You are responsible for the final judgment and acceptance (Human in the loop)

Summary of this article

You have now accomplished the three most important things:

  • Version prerequisites confirmed
  • MCP capability is enabled in Xcode.
  • Successfully connected using Gemini CLI and verified tool visibility.

In the next article, we'll jump straight into practical application: using a high-quality target hint word, we'll have the Agent collaboratively generate a "playable" SwiftUI + SpriteKit mini-project, and explain how to let it fix compilation errors on its own.

References

  • Peter Friese: Agentic Coding in Xcode with Gemini CLI
    https://peterfriese.dev/blog/2026/agentic-coding-xcode-geminicli/
  • Apple: Xcode 26.3 unlocks the power of agentic coding
    https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/02/xcode-26-point-3-unlocks-the-power-of-agentic-coding/
  • Apple Docs: Setting up coding intelligence
    https://developer.apple.com/documentation/Xcode/setting-up-coding-intelligence
  • Apple Docs: Giving external agentic coding tools access to Xcode
    https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/giving-agentic-coding-tools-access-to-xcode
  • Gemini CLI release v0.27.3
    https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli/releases/tag/v0.27.3
